ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, medical applications and wearable technology have captured attention. In fact, the importance of the wearable thermoelectric devices in the medical applications renders to their potentiality to produce electricity from the difference between the surroundings and the human body temperatures. So, the design of self-sustained wearable devices based on energy harvesting has been established worldwide for these goals. Thermoelectric generators are a favorable choice among different types of harvesting sources, such as radio frequency, light, and others, thanks to the independence of their light environments or their wearable activity which facilitates to be used in medical field. Indeed, the thermoelectric generators have many advantages such as (1) easiness and/or no maintenance (2) light weight (3) small size (4) high safety and reliability (5) free of undesired fluids and noise (6) flexible (7) and stable parts [1]. It is known that the most important component in the thermoelectric generators is the thermoelectric materials which have been acquired much attention in wearable electronic devices due to their abilities to generate electrical energy from the waste heat.

For thermoelectric materials, the efficiency of the energy conversion can be determined from a dimensionless figure of merit quantity, which is stated from the relation ZT = σS
2
T/k where T = temperature in kelvin, S = the Seebeck coefficient, σS 2 = power factor (PF), and k and σ = thermal and electrical conductivities, respectively [2]. However, researches proved that it is difficult to acquire thermoelectric materials with a large ZT value, due to the difficulty to improve the three thermoelectric parameters (S, σ, and k) simultaneously. This is because of the surge in σ produces an increase in k, and the surge in S produces a decrease in σ and so on [3]. Up to now, the theoretical and experimental works have presented two promising strategies to remarkably improve the values of the figure of merit. The first strategy is based on using thermoelectric materials with low dimensions like (nanotube, nanowire and others) due to the effect of quantum size of these materials [4]. The second strategy depends on using thermoelectric materials with a new phonon glass electron crystal like (some composites, skutterudites and others), owing to their unique components and structures [4,5]. A large number of literatures have been performed for the commercial thermoelectric materials based on inorganic materials, (Bi2Te3-based alloys and others), due to their high thermoelectric performance at room temperature [6–10]. However, researchers reported many shortcomings for these inorganic materials such as inflexibility, contamination with heavy metal, costly preparation methods, heavy thermoelectric devices, complicated manufacturing on a large-scale and high-cost of raw materials, which obstacle their applications in this field. To avoid these problems, thermoelectric materials with environmental friendly features, affordability, flexibility and high thermoelectric efficiency becomes more demanded with the accelerating progress in wearable and bendable electronic generators [11–13]. To address these points, organic conductive polymers (particularly, PEDOT:PSS) were introduced as flexible materials with high thermoelectric performance due to their low cost, mass fabrication using different types of printers such as (roll-to-roll printing), environmentally benign, abundant, affordability, visibility, high processability, flexibility, low k, and being intrinsically high σ [1–3,14,15]. In fact, the conductive PEDOT:PSS polymers were applied alone as a thermoelectric material, however, they exhibited a very weak thermoelectric performance when compared to inorganics. This is due to the oxidization level of PEDOT which is very difficult to be controlled yet, and the unstable PEDOT redox state properties. Moreover, PEDOT:PSS has a high electrical conductivity (1–10 S/cm) but its Seebeck coefficient is still low (14–18 μV/K), which hinders its applications [16]. Then this polymer (PEDOT:PSS) has been treated with base and/or acid materials to improve its thermoelectric functions, e.g., the thermoelectric PEDOT:PSS polymers have been treated with (5% DMSO and PEO) to improve its parameters (PF = 157.35 μW/mK2, S = 40 μV/K, and σ = 1300 S/cm) at 0.3% of PEO [17]. Also, the hydrazine treated PEDOT:PSS to achieve a PF = 115.5 μW/mK2, S = 32–44 μV/K and σ = 400–800 S/cm [18]. Although the above works reported how to enhance the thermoelectric performance of PEDOT:PSS, but this conductive polymer is still suffering from some shortcomings such as its hard solubility in solvents, complicated methods of preparation which were usually blended in water with PSS polymer and this leads to restricting their applications [19,20]. Moreover, the high charge carriers of this conductive polymers create the growth in the electronic thermal conductivity (κ e), and hence growing the total thermal conductivity, κ = κ p + κ e. On the other hand, PEDOT:PSS polymer has been recently mixed with inorganic materials to enhance the thermoelectric parameters [21]. For example, a maximum σ = 421 S/cm and PF = 9.9 μW/mK2 were obtained for the system (PEDOT:PSS/Bi2Te3 10 wt%) to achieve a ZT = 0.04 at room temperature (R.T). This lower PF and ZT values are attributed to the used Bi2Te3 particles (325 mesh) are in micrometer sized, which make a poor dispersion in the PEDOT:PSS [22]. The nano-scale Bi2Te3 was physical mixed with PEDOT:PSS to obtain PEDOT:PSS/Bi2Te3 nano-composite for a PF = 131 μW m−1K−2 and a ZT = 0.08 at 300 K [23]. Also, the system PEDOT:PSS/Bi0.5Sb1.5Te3 nanosheets with (4.1 wt%, size 125–800 nm) were designed to obtain a PF = 32.26 μW m−1K−2 and a ZT = 0.05 at R.T [24]. Moreover, the functionalized Te nanorods were used to obtain the composite (PEDOT:PSS/functionalized Te nanorods) for a PF = 70.9 μW m−1K−2, and a ZT = 0.10 at R.T [25]. Also, the PEDOT:PSS conducting polymers mixed with carbon materials for adjusting and tuning the electrical conductivity and Seebeck coefficient. For example, the ZT value of PEDOT:PSS increased to be (0.02) for the system (PEDOT:PSS/SWNT 35 wt%) [26]. Also, the fabrication of PEDOT:PSS/PANI/MWCNT nano-composite was done by using the layer-by-layer assembly technique to gain a higher S = 120 μVK−1, a high σ = 19 × 102 S cm−1, and a PF = 2710 μW m−1K−2 at 80 quad layers [27]. However, these thermoelectric materials are still suffering from many problems until now, such as (1) the energy filtering effect, (2) weak active interface thermoelectric regions between the filler and polymer (electrical contacts) as well as between the interconnect regions for device application, (3) selecting appropriate fillers, fillers/polymers materials, and nano-scaled fillers/polymers composites [28]. Thus to address aforementioned limitations and needs, with bearing in mind all the problems and desires, we introduce new flexible and freestanding nanocomposites constructed by silver decorated nitrogen doped carbon dots (Ag@NCDots) incorporated into poly(3,4-ethylene-dioxythiophene):poly(styrenesulphonate)/poly(vinyl alcohol), PEDOT:PSS/PVA, (Ag@NCDots/PPVA) for high mechanical parameters, high conductive and high thermoelectric performance are available until now, to the best of our survey.

Actually, PEDOT:PSS is a conducting polymer with excellent performances has been applied in many functional applications such as optical and organic devices [5], multifunctional fabric [6], conducting textiles, organic electronics and sensors [5–7], wearable bionics devices, and many other technologies which are coming from its tunable electrical conductivity. On the other hand, PVA is one of the most attractive polymeric materials for functional applications thanks to its special properties such as its environmental stability, processability, and reversible dominance of its thermoelectric performance [29]. Also, carbon dots (CDots) have appeared as a unique zero-dimensional materials owing to their easy processability, fast preparation, high dispersion because of their functionalized groups and their special properties. Recently, CDots have been used as a high thermoelectric material due to they play an important role in phonon scattering [30,31]. Moreover, silver as conducting particles were embedded in different crystalline, amorphous and polymeric materials to improve its electrical, mechanical and other properties [32,33]. In fact, PEDOT:PSS/Ag nanowires system has been fabricated for thermoelectric applications. It was found that the PF = 2.86 μW/mK2 and a ZT = 0.002 [34]. Also, (PEDOT:PSS/PVA) system has been constructed and displayed σ = 15.5 S/cm, k = 0.28 Wm/K, S = 17.8 μV/K, and ZT = 0.008 at R.T [5]. Also, El-Shamy recently has reported on the PEDOT:PSS/CDots system for thermoelectric application [4]. This system showed a high σ = 632.6 S/cm, S = 42.2 μV/K, k = 0.27 Wm/K and PF = 116.2 μW/mK2 at R.T [4]. Then El-Shamy treated this system with H2SO4 acid to improve the thermoelectric parameters. It was found that the optimum conditions at 10 M of H2SO4 acid, where at this H2SO4 concentration the σ, S, k and PF parameters dramatically increased to 1753.92 S/cm, 30 μV/K, 0.37 Wm/K and 148.27 μW/mK2 at R.T, respectively [4]. However, these composites have been limited by the higher thermal conductivity with the increase in metal and/or CDots concentration [4].

Herein, we report an easy, affordable and fast synthesis of Ag@NCDots/PPVA nano-composite by using casting techniques supported by hydrothermal synthesizing NCDots decorated with Ag nano-particles by precipitation method for the thermoelectric power performance for the first time. Actually, PEDOT:PSS polymer was chosen here because of its high electrical conductivity, easy doping tunability, its brilliant thermal stability, good thermoelectric power functions such as low intrinsic k value, and having a good conjugated degree and high planarity in its backbones, which facilitates the interaction with PVA and Ag@NCDots. Also, PVA polymer was chosen in this work due to its good adhesive property, capability of binding with lower thermal conductive properties, its high flexibility and mechanical properties. The embedding of PVA into PEDOT:PSS polymer was done in this work to (i) reduce and control the thermal conductivity of the composite, and also (ii) PVA (-OH groups) interacts with PEDOT:PSS by the hydrogen H-bonding resulting in promoting crosslinking density and hence improving the mechanical parameters of PEDOT:PSS. On the other hand, Ag@NCDots nano-particles as fillers were significantly selected to improve the conductivity and the thermoelectric properties. NCDots nano-particles have been selected due to (i) their surface defects and their surface functional groups (-OH/-NH and –COO–), which can easily interact with PPVA through a strong π-π conjugated interfacial interaction that can improve the thermoelectric functions. (ii) Producing a gaseous state of nitrogen which fill the voids and defects in the nanocomposite and decline the thermal conductivity. The doping of CDots with nitrogen and decorated with Ag were done to (i) increase the electrical conductivity, and (ii) they lead to breaking the CDots crystal structure into small fragments by transformation of sp2 carbon atom to sp3 carbon atoms. These fragments, edges and defects together produce a phonon scattering in Ag@NCDots particles, reducing the amount of heat passing through Ag@NCDots and thus decreasing the thermal conductivity in the Ag@NCDots/PPVA nano-composite.

PEDOT:PSS (CLEVIOS PH1000) with concentration 1.3 wt%, Polyvinyl alcohol (M w = 104 g/mol), Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.5%), Acrylic acid (99.9%), Ethylenediamine (99.9%), silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.8%), Sodium Borohydride (NBH4, 99.9%) and Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH, 99.9%) were provided from the Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals.

PEDOT:PSS/PVA (PPVA) films were designed by the casting approach [4,36–39], See Supporting Information S1. Also, NCDots nanoparticles were synthesized by using the hydrothermal approach [4,36–39], See Supporting Information S2. On the other hand, Ag@NCDots nanoparticles were synthesized through the precipitation method [4,36–39], See Supporting Information S3.

The nanocomposite Ag@NCDots/PEDOT:PSS/PVA (Ag@NCDots/PPVA) films were designed by using the casting approach [4,36–39], (see Scheme 1 ) . The nanocomposite films were fabricated as follow: (Ag@NCDots) powder with different concentrations (2, 4, 6 and 8 wt%) was added to the PEDOT:PSS/PVA solution at room temperature and left for 1 h under the mechanical stirring to achieve a high degree of homogeneity. After that, the solution was poured into a petri-dish and left in a furnace for 6 h at 60 °C to obtain a dry film. A digital micrometer was used to measure the films thickness and it was found to be 220 ± 5 μm.

X-ray diffractometer (Malvern Panalytical Empyrean, UK) was applied to detect XRD patterns for the NCDots and Ag@NCDots nanoparticles with a λ = 0.154 nm of Cu Kα radiation in the 2θ range between 4° and 70° at room temperature. XPS instrument (AXIS ULTRA, Kratos Analytical) was applied to identify the elemental compositions of the nano-particles. A Nicolet DTGS TEC FTIR apparatus was applied to detect the FTIR spectra of the particles from 4000 to 400 cm−1 by using KBr pellet method. To discover the vibrational modes and study the structures of the nanoparticles, (Alpha 300R/AS from witechulm, Germany) Raman instrument was used. To study the size and shape of the nano-particles, a (JEOL-JEM-1230) TEM microscope was applied. BET instrument, model: Micromeritics ASAP 2020, USA, was applied to study the adsorption/desorption of the nano-particles under a liquid N2 environment. The mechanical parameters of the films (dimensions: 20 × 2 × 0.22 mm) were measured by using a (AMETEK) mechanical test instrument attached to a force gauge (0.01 N resolution, Hunter spring Accu force II). The work functions of the nanocomposite films have been evaluated by using the ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS pressure, model; Thermo-Fisher, Escalab 250Xi, and 5 × 10−13 13 bar) with the excitation source (He I 21.2 eV). Electrometer (Keithley 6517B) was used to measure the through plane σ ⊥ values of the films by two probe method. The measuring uncertainty of σ ⊥ was ≥5%. The in plane σ
// values were measured by four probe method. The measuring uncertainty of σ//was ≥5%. By using the van der Pauw configuration and with applying AC magnetic field, the measurements of Hall Effect (carrier concentration and mobility carriers) were evaluated.

X-ray patterns of NCDots and Ag@NCDots nanoparticles are shown in Fig. 1 a. A hump at 2θ = 23.4° was observed for the highly amorphous carbon atoms in the plane C(002) of the graphite hexagonal lattice structure of NCDots [40]. Besides this peak there are additionally four peaks were observed at 2θ = 38.5°, 44.5°, 64.7° and 77.5° according to the planes (111), (200), (220) and (311), respectively for the FCC phase of the Ag nanoparticles (JCPDS card, No. 04–0783), for Ag@NCDots sample [32,33]. These observations were confirmed by TEM measurements (Fig. 1b–f). TEM picture for NCDots nanoparticles was taken to evaluate the shape and size of NCDots (Fig. 1b). It was found that the average size of NCDots is approximately 15.4 ± 1 nm. From TEM image, it can be seen that NCDots nanoparticles have a uniform spherical shape. Also it is clear that Ag nanoparticles are decorated the NCDots surface for Ag@NCDots sample (Fig. 1c). In addition, Ag nanoparticles are distributed on the NCDots surface with a uniform manner without any change in their sizes or shapes (Fig. 1d). Fig. 1e and f shows the HRTEM micrographs of the (110) plane of NCDots with a lattice space 0.21 nm and plane (111) of Ag nanoparticles with a lattice space 0.24 nm.

To confirm the above results, FTIR spectra were performed for the NCDots and Ag@NCDots nanoparticles as a function of wavenumber (Fig. 2 a). For NCDots, there is a sharp peak at 3436 cm−1, which is attributed to the vibration of the stretching mode of (ν O–H/N–H) bonds. The band at 2920 cm−1 represents the asymmetric vibrations of the C–H bond. The vibration band of the stretching CO appeared at 1630 cm−1 and CC vibrations appeared at 1489 cm−1. In addition, the peak at 1383 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching (C–N and COO-). The band at 1044 cm−1 is ascribed to the vibration mode of the stretching C–O [41].

In case of Ag@NCDots, the FTIR spectra were shifted and their intensities were changed, proving the strong effect of Ag nanoparticles on NCDots structure. As seen, the FTIR spectra prove that the stretching vibration of groups (ν O–H/N–H) was shifted to 3280 cm−1 and its intensity decreased. Also, the peak for CO shifted to 13,730 cm−1 and its intensity decreased. Moreover, the peak for C–N/COO- shifted to 1357 cm−1. The shift in the peak position of the FTIR spectra indicates the change in the force constant according to the Hooke's law [36–38]. This means that there is a strong interaction between Ag and NCDots during the creation of Ag@NCDots nanoparticles, which indicates the change in the molecular vibrational resonance via destroying of the perfect conjugated electrons and crystal structure of NCDots through the decoration process.

In order to identify the structures and/or vibrational modes of the NCDots, and Ag@NCDots, Raman spectra were performed (Fig. 2b). Two Raman spectra have been detected for NCDots at 1610.40 and 1332.35 cm−1, respectively. The G-band was detected at ~1610.40 cm−1 thanks to the E2g of the sp2 of carbon and the D-band was detected at ~1332.35 cm−1 thanks to the vibrations of the sp3 type of carbon of defects and disordered graphite. As seen, the intensity of D-band spectrum increased for Ag@NCDots; this is attributed to the decoration of NCDots with Ag, which destroys the perfect conjugated electrons especially π-electron structure and also destroys the crystal structure, which leads to breaking the NCDots crystal structure into tiny fragments by sp3 carbon atoms. In fact, this behavior is useful in the thermoelectric performance which helps in the creation of main paths of heat transmission through NCDots [42]. These fragments, edges and defects together produce a phonon scattering in the Ag@NCDots particles, reducing the amount of heat passing through Ag@NCDots, and thus decreasing the thermal conductivity in Ag@NCDots/PPVA nanocomposite.

The elemental configurations of NCDots and Ag@NCDots particles were scrutinized by using XPS (Fig. 3 a). As seen, three spectra were detected for NCDots at Ebinding = 398.5 eV, 288.7 eV and 532.7 eV for N, C and O, respectively. As seen, the elements ratios in NCDots are 39.1%, 59.42% and 1.48% for C, N and O, respectively. Besides these spectra, a new spectrum was observed at Ebinding = 371.5 eV for Ag@NCDots and the elements ratios became 39.51%, 59.85%, 1.69% and 0.95% for C, N O and Ag, respectively, (Fig. 3a). As seen, three sharp peaks were observed for the high-resolution C1s spectrum of NCDots and these peaks are detected at Ebinding = 284.4 eV for the CC (sp2 hybridization of carbon), 285.8 eV for C–N, and 287.5 eV for C–OH groups (Fig. 3b), [30]. However, in case of Ag@NCDots, it was observed that the intensity of CC peak at Ebinding = 284.4 eV decreases, which proves the interaction of Ag with NCDots and broken NCDots to a small tiny fragments (Fig. 3c). Two bands were detected at Ebinding = 367 eV for Ag(3d5/2) and 373.5 eV for Ag(3d3/2), and these peaks were detected for the spectrum of Ag3d (Fig. 3d). More confirmation, EDX spectra were performed to identify the elements present in the obtained samples (Fig. S1). As seen, four elements were detected for C, N O, and Ag at energies 0.2, 0.3, 0.5 keV. Besides these spectra, there is another spectrum at 2 keV for S element which is attributed to the PEDOT:PSS for the Ag@NCDots/PPVA composite.

In order to study the adsorption/desorption analysis of Ag@NCDots nano-particles, BET analysis was performed under a stream of nitrogen (Fig. 4 ). As shown, BET analysis showed a large surface area of 496 m2/g. On the other side, the N2 adsorption–desorption of Ag@NCDots nanoparticles proved that the isotherm is a typical IV with a hysteresis loop H3, confirming the presence of meso and macro-pores structure [31]. Such structure leads to facilitating the adsorption of organic PPVA molecules on the surface of Ag@NCDots which help in reducing the thermal conductivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermoelectric power performance of Ag@NCDots/PPVA nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: We offer in this work an efficient way to improve the ZT value of the PEDOT:PSS polymer by reducing/controlling its k value. The scheme of this work is based on decorating NCDots with Ag nano-particles and then incorporated into the matrix of PEDOT:PSS/PVA (PPVA) to produce Ag@NCDots/PPVA films. The author expects that the strong interfacial π-π conjugated interactions will be established between the PPVA polymer and the Ag@NCDots which is reflected in enhancing the mechanical, electrical and thermoelectric functions of the films. In fact, the anisotropic (in plane//and through plane ⊥) thermoelectric functions has been extensively studied.

Fig. 5 shows the k ∥ and k ⊥ values as a function of Ag@NCDots. As seen, the k ∥ values slightly increased and recorded 0.35 ± 0.01 W/mK at 8 w.% Ag@NCDots. Alternatively, the k ⊥ values reduced and recorded 0.195 ± 0.01 W/mK at 8 w.%. Return to Fig. 5, the k ∥ values are figured with the Ag@NCDots concentration. As seen, the k ∥ ≈ 1.2k ⊥ for the obtained PPVA. In fact, an anisotropic k values with k ∥ ≈ 2k ⊥ and k ∥ ≈ 3k ⊥ have been reported for the drop casted PEDOT:PSS and PEDOT:PSS/DMSO, respectively, (reported by Liu et al. [43]). Also, the anisotropic k values with k ⊥ = 0.30 ± 0.02 W/mK and k ∥ = 0.42 ± 0.07 W/mK have been reported for the spin-cast PEDOT:PSS/DMSO film (thickness ≈1.4 μm) when σ ≈ 620 S/cm [44]. Also, the anisotropic σ with (σ ∥ ≈ 820 S/cm, σ ⊥ ≈ 36 S/cm) and k values with (k ∥ = 0.84 ± 0.33 W/mK, k ⊥ = 0.15 ± 0.04 W/mK) have been reported for the (3 wt%) EG/PEDOT:PSS at a film thickness ≈ 100 μm [45]. As seen, the embedding of PVA into the PEDOT:PSS polymer lead to reducing and controlling the anisotropic thermal conductivity as compared with the previous reported results. Return to our data profile, it was found that with the growth of Ag@NCDots concentration, the k ∥ values are slightly increased with a narrow range of variation and the reason behind that is the preferred orientation of PSS polymer chains to align in the in plane direction during the process of water evaporation which alignment the polymer molecules parallel to the surface of the film [45,46]. Also, the k ∥ value is smaller than that recently reported literatures, and this is can be attributed to the strong chemically bonding between the Ag@NCDots nanoparticles and the organic PPVA molecules [47]. The analogue between the recent reported k ∥ and k ⊥ values of conducting polymeric films and their anisotropic ratio (k ∥/k ⊥) has been recorded in Table 1 . From table, our k ⊥ value is smaller than the reported before which refers to the role of Ag@NCDots and PVA in reducing the k ⊥ values. Also, the k ∥ value is lower than the reported k ∥ values except Ref. [35]. The Ag@NCDots/PPVA has an anisotropic (k ∥/k ⊥) ratio = 1.8, which indicates to impact of the film thickness on the thermal conductivity.

Alternatively, The through plane thermal conductivity (k ⊥) of Ag@NCDots/PPVA with different concentration of Ag@NCDots is represented in Fig. 5. As seen, the k ⊥ of the PPVA films is 0.26 ± 0.003 W/mK and this value is reduced to be 0.23 ± 0.006 W/mK when inserting 2 wt% of Ag@NCDots. This value of k ⊥ is reduced to be 0.195 ± 0.007 W/mK at 4 wt% of Ag@NCDots and this value remains constant without any change with increasing the Ag@NCDots concentration. As seen, no changes have been observed for the k ⊥ of Ag@NCDots/PPVA with growing Ag@NCDots concentrations from 4 to 8 wt% at room temperature.

In fact, the mechanism of the thermal conductivity depends on three factors. (i) The relatively small size of Ag@NCDots nanoparticles and the defects on their surfaces, in addition, the low thermal conductivity of PVA (0.12–0.27 W/mK) [54], PEDOT:PSS (0.2 W/mK) [55] and PEDOT:PSS/PVA limits the heat transmission between Ag@NCDots particles and hence reducing the thermal conductivity of Ag@NCDots/PPVA nanocomposite [56]. (ii) sp2 carbon atoms are transformed into sp3 carbon atoms just when doping CDots nanoparticles with nitrogen and decorated with Ag atoms, which destroy the perfect conjugated electrons especially π-electron structure and also destroying the crystal structure, which are responsible for the creation of main paths of heat transmission through NCDots [42]. As reported earlier [4,37], the main carriers of heat transfer in NCDots are phonons, which are responsible for delivering heat by vibration of lattice. When decorating Ag into NCDots, the NCDots crystal structure is broken into tiny fragments by sp3 carbon atoms (i.e, chemically connected carbon atoms, see XPS spectra). These obstacles (fragments), edges and defects together produce a phonon scattering in the Ag@NCDots particles, reducing the amount of heat passing through Ag@NCDots, and thus decreasing the thermal conductivity in Ag@NCDots/PPVA [57]. (iii) Doping of CDots with nitrogen and decorated with Ag particles creates more and more pores in CDots surface that fill with PPVA polymer chains, see BET analysis, or non-flowing air/nitrogen with low k values, which can prevent heat conduction [58,59]. When the air/nitrogen fills these pores and voids in the matrix makes interfaces (air, nitrogen/composites), besides the interfaces (Ag@NCDots/PPVA) which are already present in the nanocomposite. These interfaces create the phonons mismatch which produces a phonon scattering at the interfaces and hence producing a very low κ p and hence reducing the k. Du et al. demonstrated that polymer composite has a low thermal conductivity and its thermal conductivity remains constant even when doped with a high CNTs concentration due to the porous structure [60]. The phonon propagation was blocked by the phonon scattering at the interface that produces low heat conduction. Similarly, Suemori et al. reported a low thermal conductivity of (PS/75 wt% CNTs) composite thanks to a numerous amount of voids [61]. In our work, it is clear that the addition of Ag@NCDots particles (Ag and/or N) has a major effect on decreasing/controlling the thermal conductivity. (iv) The decorating of Ag on the NCDots surface creates some discontinuity in the system and makes Ag@NCDots/PPVA interfaces, so, a large phonon scattering was produced at these interfaces due to the incomplete contact between PPVA and the nanoparticles, producing a low thermal conductivity [62,63]. (v) Electrons can transfer heat in CDots via the travelling of electrons. The doping the CDots with nitrogen and decorated with Ag leads to breaking the conjugated structures (especially π-electrons) and this leads to increasing the resistance of the electron motions. Thus, reducing the heat transferred by travelling of electrons and hence reducing the k e value. As follow, the k ⊥ values of the nano-composite reduced due to the decrease in k e. In our work, it is noticeable that the values of k ⊥ are under control, which gives the nanocomposite a chance to be used as a thermoelectric material [64]. It can be concluded that, this nanocomposite maintains a low thermal conductivity due to numerous phonon scattering boundaries produced from the interfaces between PPVA and Ag@NCDots nanoparticles inside the nanocomposite matrix, which leads to suppressing the phonon transport [4,12,36,39]. In fact, the thermal conductivity κ can be described by the relation: κ = κ e + κ p, where κ e is the electron thermal conductivity and κ p is the phonon thermal conductivity. As we discuss, both κ p and κ e reduced and controlled with the presence of Ag@NCDots inside the matrix which in turns lead to decreasing the k ⊥. In fact, the obtained k ⊥ value for our material is (0.195 W/mK) which is smaller than that of PPVA (0.26 W/mK) in this study and the previously reported k ⊥ values such as graphene/PEDOT:PSS (0.30 W/mK) [65], 4 wt% MoS2/PEDOT:PSS (0.38 W/mK) [66], Te NWs/PEDOT:PSS (0.22 W/mK) [67] and 29.6 wt% Ge/PEDOT:PSS (0.50 W/mK) [68].

Fig. 6 presents the σ ∥ and σ ⊥ of the said films as a function of Ag@NCDots concentration. As detected, the σ ∥ value is 249.3 ± 22 S/cm for PPVA. Also, the σ ∥ value slightly increased from 258.3 ± 18 S/cm to 287.4 ± 19 S/cm with the growth of the Ag@NCDots concentration from 2 to 8 wt%, and the reason behind this is the well-packed of PEDOT nano-crystals in the films. These PEDOT layer structures are ordered in the in-plane direction which creates the carrier localization length greater than molecular spacing and producing a large σ ∥ values.

Fig. 6 shows the σ ⊥ of the said films as a function of Ag@NCDots concentration. As shown, the σ ⊥ increases with the increase in the Ag@NCDots concentration because of the creation of the conductive paths of Ag@NCDots nanoparticles inside the PPVA matrix [4,37]. The σ ⊥ of PPVA was found to be 22.9 ± 9 S/cm and this low value of the conductivity is due to the insulating behavior of PVA [32,33]. But with mixing PPVA with Ag@NCDots (2 wt%), the σ ⊥ raised to 55.9 ± 10 S/cm. Also, with increasing the Ag@NCDots concentrations in PPVA, the σ ⊥ increased and reached 181.7 ± 11 S/cm at 8 wt% Ag@NCDots.

Generally, the improvement of the conductivity with increasing Ag@NCDots in PPVA is illustrated on the basis of carrier's mobility and carrier concentration, where the electrical conductivity can be expressed by Equation:
σ
=
n e μ
Where n is the carriers concentration, e is the electronic charge and μ is the carriers mobility. Fig. 7 shows that the carrier's concentration and the carrier's mobility increased gradually with increasing the Ag@NCDots concentrations, and this agrees with the increase in conductivity. Indeed, the presence of Ag@NCDots inside the polymer matrix leads to facilitating the charge transfer complex between the Ag@NCDots (via their functional groups) and the polymeric chains which leads to an increase in the absorption/desorption of the polymer chains to the Ag@NCDots surface [4,31]. Therefore, strong interactions (π-π conjugated interaction type) are created between Ag@NCDots and the polymer chains and this leads to the strong adhesion of the polymer chains on the Ag@NCDots surface. As a result, the polymeric molecular conformation is expanded and the chains are packed in a high order during the increase in the adsorbed polymeric chains on the Ag@NCDots. This behavior leads to the ring twisting of polymer which in turns reduces the π-π conjugated defects in the polymer backbone and rises the electron delocalization degree and hence reduces the barrier of the hopping carriers [69,70] and thus increasing the carrier mobilities in Ag@NCDots/PPVA. Also, the strong π-π interactions of Ag@NCDots with PPVA chains lead to bridging the carrier transport, which increases the carrier mobility, and consequently increasing the σ values. Therefore the increase in electrical conductivity can be attributed to the presence of Ag and NCDots in PPVA and the strong adhesion of Ag@NCDots with PPVA segments/chains which creates a conductive pathway of Ag@NCDots nanoparticles in the template of the nanocomposite. These two phenomena raise the charge transfer channels by decreasing the carrier jump barrier, and hence increasing the conductivity of the nanocomposites [71,72].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Seebeck effect and power factor studies
ce:section.ce:para: In fact, the S is originated from; Electrons with high thermal velocities diffuse from the hot (Ag@NCDots/PPVA) end to the cold (Ag@NCDots/PPVA) end with lower thermal velocities when the both sides of Ag@NCDots/PPVA nanocomposites are exposed to different temperatures. So, the electromotive forces (emf) were generated between the two ends. From the results, S has a positive value in all measured temperature ranges, this means that the nanocomposite behaves like a p-type semiconductor and the holes are the carrier's majority in this nanocomposite [4,37]. The increase in the Seebeck values is attributed to (i) the growth of the Ag@NCDots in the films which leads to pushing the Fermi level away from the valence band [4,36,39]. In fact, the increase in the Ag@NCDots concentration inside the PPVA matrix leads to increasing the interfaces between PPVA segments and Ag@NCDots inside the system and producing a potential barrier of energy that leads to creating a carrier energy-filtering effect which allow passing the carriers with high-energies and blocking those of the lower energies [4]. This in turns produces an increase in the main carrier's energies in carrier transport and therefore growing the Seebeck coefficient [36]. (ii) Moreover, nano-scale dimension of both Ag and NCDots leads to creating a quantum confinement through the carrier's transport, and therefore growing the Seebeck coefficient [37]. To more illustration of the energy filtering effect, the work functions (φ) of Ag@NCDots nanoparticles and PPVA were measured by UPS spectroscopy. They found to be 4.85 and 4.71 eV, respectively. From the interfacial band diagrams (Scheme 2 ), the energy barrier at the Ag@NCDots/PPVA interface is 0.14 eV. This potential barrier of energy leads to creating a carrier energy-filtering effect, which allow passing the carriers with high-energies and blocking those of the lower energies.

The power factor (PF) of Ag@NCDots/PPVA films can be evaluated from the relation PF = σ S 2. By breaking this relation down into
P F
=
( −
k B
e
log
p
N μ
)
2
, where
k B
is the Boltzmann constant, e is the electronic charge,
N μ
is the effective density of states of transport level,
p
is the carrier concentration of hole and carrier mobility
μ
. From the equation it can be seen that the power factor is strongly depends on the carrier mobility
μ
. It is believed that the power factor increases with the growth of Ag@NCDots concentration, which in turns increases the carrier's concentration and carrier's mobility. The growth in PF values is attributed to the growth in the conductivity and the Seebeck coefficient of Ag@NCDots/PPVA. The increase in the conductivity of Ag@NCDots/PPVA is attributed to the strong interactions between Ag@NCDots and the adjacent segments of PPVA matrix. This interaction structure facilitates the ability to form conductive paths from Ag@NCDots nanoparticles for enabling the carrier transport. In fact, a large number of interfaces between PPVA segments and Ag@NCDots inside the Ag@NCDots/PPVA system produce a potential barrier of energy that leads to creating a carrier energy-filtering effect [4]. It is believed that the high energy carrier is involved in the transport mechanism while the carriers with lower energy are scattered at the interfaces, so the filtering by means of potential barrier of nano-scales can enhance the PF values. The carrier mobility also increased in this Ag@NCDots/PPVA with raising the Ag@NCDots concentrations. These results show that the enhancement of the carrier's mobility by means of energy filtering allows increasing the value of Seebeck with keeping the conductivity values without destroying, therefore PF value of Ag@NCDots/PPVA increases [37]. As observed, the obtained PF //(123.54 ± 6 μW/mK2 at 8 wt%) values in this work is larger than the reported PF //~60.05 μW/mK2 for Te–Bi2Te3/PEDOT:PSS system [73], 51.4 μW/mK2 for PEDOT:PSS/PC-Te system [74] and 84 μW/mK2 for Te–Cu1.75Te/PEDOT:PSS system [75]. However the PF //value of our system is still smaller than the reported PF //~206 μW/mK2 for SSWNTs/PEDOT:PSS/Te system [76] and 143 μW/mK2 for rGO/PEDOT:PSS/Te system [77].

Table 2 shows the analogy between our thermoelectric materials and the other thermoelectric materials based on polymeric structures. A higher ZT value was obtained for our materials than that of some previously reported materials. From this analogy, we concluded that, our thermoelectric materials have some features such as being economical, bendable, consuming small amount of raw materials, environmentally friendly, high safety, fast processability, high mechanical and thermal stabilities as well as their easy preparation, when compared with other polymeric materials. Alternatively, our material showed some lower Seebeck coefficient values in comparison with other reported materials. In conclusion, it can be recommended that, the manipulation/treatment of these materials with acid or base materials may improve the electrical conductivity and Seebeck coefficient of these materials and hence boosting the thermoelectric parameters of these materials based on these nano-hybrid fillers.

Taking the benefits of the fabricated thermoelectric Ag@NCDots/PPVA material such as (flexibility, facile preparation, self-supporting, rolling up, and brilliant thermoelectric parameters), engineering of thermoelectric generator is very easy and the fabricated generator has the above benefits, and displays superior performance. Number of thermoelectric legs (12 legs) is electrically connected with silver conductor paste in series connection to create a thermoelectric generator in order to increase the V
output , as shown in Fig. S2. The thermoelectric voltage (V
output ) produced by the engineered generator as a function of ΔT (a hot plate was used to obtain ΔT) is depicted in Fig. 11 .

As seen, with the increase in ΔT, the output thermo-voltage (V) increases thanks to the Seebeck effect. Besides, with the increase in the ΔT, the output thermo-voltage (V) increases and reaches 15.12 mV at ΔT = 16 K for 12 legs. It is clear that there are differences between the experimental (V Exp = 15.12 mV) at applied ΔT = 16 K, and the theoretical value of the V Th according to the relation:
V
=
Δ T × S × N
, where S represents the Seebeck coefficient, and N represents the number of the legs. These differences are attributed to the heat loss produced by the Ohmic contact between the legs and the wires, as well as between the legs and the Ag paste. However, the achieved results show the possibility of increasing the generator performance by increasing the number of the legs. The open-circuit voltage and power of the generator figured as a function of the current. As seen, at the steady-state temperature difference ΔT = 16 K, the output power (~15.59 nW) was experimentally achieved. Indeed, a minimum value of the output voltage needed for any small wearable device to work is 1.5 V. However, the obtained output voltage (15.12 mV) is acceptable when compared to other reported thermoelectric devices (Table 3 ). The output voltage (15.12 mV at a load resistance 720 Ω) in this work is obtained at a small ΔT = 16 K and 12 legs, which can be increased by increasing the number of legs and/or by increasing the temperature difference ΔT. Moreover, the output power Poutput can be further improved through adapting the structure of the device, increasing the number of legs and/or combining flexible materials of N-type with high thermoelectric performance to assemble P–N type thermoelectric one.

Fig. 12 a shows the stress-strain curve of the Ag@NCDots/PPVA films. The mechanical parameters of Ag@NCDots/PPVA were studied as a function of the concentration of Ag@NCDots (Fig. 12c and d). As shown, the growth in Ag@NCDots concentrations leads to increasing the Young's modulus as well as the tensile strength and reducing the strain at break. This is maybe due to the growth of the cross-linking densities in the nano-composites [91–94]. In fact, the PVA (-OH groups) interacts with both the PEDOT:PSS chains through the hydrogen H-bonding and facilitates the interaction of Ag@NCDots and PPVA (Fig. 12b). Also, these cross-linking densities increase when increasing the concentration of Ag@NCDots. In fact, the larger cross-linking densities inside Ag@NCDots/PPVA, leads to lowering the probability of chains/segments orientation, producing a higher strength, and hence increasing the Young's modulus of the composite [95–98]. The growth in the cross-linking densities produces an increase in the thermal and mechanical stabilities of the nano-composites. Moreover, the decline in the strain was observed with increasing Ag@NCDots concentrations (Fig. 12d). In fact, the decrease in the strain is ascribed to (i) the increase in the cross-linking densities inside the films. (ii) The decline in the polymer chains unravel, which are aligning perpendicular to the direction of the applied stress and leads to a decrease in the plastic region. It can be seen that the enhancement of mechanical parameters of the said thermoelectric nanocomposite fulfill the request of standard of ASTM
D1424
(4–70 LBS) and can satisfy the requirements of thermoelectric device fabrication.

Abdullah F. Al Naim: Review; Measurements, Software, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Sobhy S. Ibrahim: Review, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization.
